title: Correcting a Faulty Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Implementation

statement.markdown: You are given two integers, a and b. The function must return their greatest common divisor (GCD). The GCD of two integers is the largest positive integer that divides both numbers without leaving a remainder.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer a. The second line contains an integer b.
statement.outputFormat: The GCD of a and b should be printed on a new line.
statement.notes: You can use the Euclidean algorithm for this problem.

hints.text: Use the Euclidean algorithm to find the GCD.

editorial.title: Correcting a Faulty Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Implementation
editorial.intuition: The Euclidean algorithm is an efficient way to calculate the GCD of two numbers.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve checking all numbers up to the minimum of a and b to see if they divide both numbers.
editorial.betterSolution: Using the Euclidean algorithm, we can repeatedly apply the division algorithm to find the GCD.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses the Euclidean algorithm with a recurrence relation.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the Euclidean algorithm can be proven using mathematical induction.
editorial.dryRun: Let's run the Euclidean algorithm on two random numbers to see how it works.
commonMistakes: Not using the return statement correctly
Not handling the edge case where a is 0 and b is 0

functionSignature.java: public int gcd(int a, int b);
